abstract.para: To run a virtualization server (&vmhost;) that can host one or more guest systems (&vmguest;s), you need to install required virtualization components on the server.

section.title: Introduction
section.para: To run a virtualization server (&vmhost;) that can host one or more guest systems (&vmguest;s), you need to install required virtualization components on the server. These components vary depending on which virtualization technology you want to use.

section.title: Installing virtualization components
section.para: You can install the virtualization tools required to run a &vmhost; in one of the following ways:

listitem.para: By selecting a specific system role during &productname; installation on the &vmhost;
By installing specific installation patterns on an already installed and running &productname;.

section.title: Specifying a system role
section.para: You can install all the tools required for virtualization during the installation of &productname; on the &vmhost;. During the installation, you are presented with the System Role screen.
Here you can select the KVM Virtualization Host role. The appropriate software selection and setup is automatically performed during &productname; installation.

tip.para: Both virtualization system roles create a dedicated /var/lib/libvirt partition, and enable the &firewalld; and &kdump; services.

section.title: Installing specific installation patterns
section.para: Related software packages from &productname; software repositories are organized into installation patterns. You can use these patterns to install specific virtualization components on an already running &productname;. Use zypper to install them:
To install the &kvm; environment, consider the following patterns:

listitem.para: Installs basic &vmhost; with the &kvm; and &qemu; environments.
Installs &libvirt; tools for managing and monitoring &vmguest;s in &kvm; environment.

section.title: Enable nested virtualization in &kvm;

important.title: Technology preview
important.para: &kvm;'s nested virtualization is still a technology preview. It is provided for testing purposes and is not supported.

section.para: Nested guests are &kvm; guests run in a &kvm; guest. When describing nested guests, we use the following virtualization layers:

listitem.para: A virtual machine running on L0. Because it can run another &kvm;, it is called a guest hypervisor.
A virtual machine running on L1. It is called a nested guest.

section.para: Nested virtualization has many advantages. You can benefit from it in the following scenarios:

listitem.para: Manage your own virtual machines directly with your hypervisor of choice in cloud environments.
Enable the live migration of hypervisors and their guest virtual machines as a single entity.

note.para: Live migration of a nested &vmguest; is not supported.

listitem.para: Use it for software development and testing.

section.para: To enable nesting temporarily, remove the module and reload it with the nested &kvm; module parameter:

listitem.para: For Intel CPUs, edit /etc/modprobe.d/kvm_intel.conf and add the following line:
For AMD CPUs, edit /etc/modprobe.d/kvm_amd.conf and add the following line:

section.para: When your L0 host is capable of nesting, you can start an L1 guest in one of the following ways:

listitem.para: Use the -cpu host &qemu; command line option.
Add the vmx (for Intel CPUs) or the svm (for AMD CPUs) CPU feature to the -cpu &qemu; command line option, which enables virtualization for the virtual CPU.

section.title: &esx; as a guest hypervisor
section.para: If you use &esx; as a guest hypervisor on top of a &kvm; bare metal hypervisor, you may experience unstable network communication. This problem occurs especially between nested &kvm; guests and the &kvm; bare metal hypervisor or external network. The following default CPU configuration of the nested &kvm; guest is causing the problem:
To fix it, modify the CPU configuration as follow:
